did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep neural network (DNN)-based diagnostic algorithm to differentiate skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) from benign lesions using laser-induced plasma spectroscopy (LIPS) spectra. The DNN was trained and evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation to obtain diagnostic accuracy metrics (sensitivity and specificity).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a multisite clinical study involving 353 patients, where in vivo LIPS spectra were acquired and analyzed using a deep neural network to determine diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity 94.6%, specificity 88.9%). It includes specific methods, results, and safety evaluations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Real-time, in vivo skin cancer triage by laser-induced plasma spectroscopy combined with a deep learning-based diagnostic algorithm
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy and safety of a real-time, in vivo skin cancer triage system combining laser-induced plasma spectroscopy (LIPS) with a deep neural network (DNN). The multisite clinical study recruited 353 patients from Australian clinics, acquiring LIPS spectra from 296 skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) and 316 benign lesions. The DNN-based diagnostic algorithm was validated using 10-fold cross-validation to differentiate malignant from benign lesions. The system achieved a sensitivity of 94.6% (95% CI: 92.0%-97.2%) and a specificity of 88.9% (95% CI: 85.5%-92.4%). No adverse events or histological evidence of tissue damage were observed, indicating the procedure's safety. The authors conclude that this LIPS-DNN system is a promising tool for high-accuracy, real-time skin cancer triage in clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: 186 lesions included in the study; confirmed by histopathology.
96 lesions included in the study (79 SCC + 17 SCC in situ); confirmed by histopathology.
14 lesions included in the study (13 melanoma + 1 melanoma in situ); confirmed by histopathology.
316 benign lesions total; 165 confirmed by histopathology, 151 confirmed visually by physicians.
Multiple subtypes included; counts vary by subtype (e.g., Compound nevus: 27 lesions, 26 biopsied).
Various benign conditions with small counts (1-4 lesions each); most confirmed by histopathology or visual confirmation.

all_task_types.target: Differentiation of skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) from benign lesions.
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing malignant skin cancers from benign lesions using a deep neural network (DNN) based on laser-induced plasma spectroscopy (LIPS) spectra. The model output was thresholded at 0.5 for the probability of malignancy to determine the class label. Diagnostic performance was evaluated using sensitivity (94.6%) and specificity (88.9%).

datasets_used.name: Multisite Clinical Study Dataset (Australian Clinics)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset collected from a multisite clinical study involving 353 patients recruited from three dermatology and skin cancer clinics in Australia: Eastern Suburbs Dermatology (Bondi Junction), Newcastle Skin Check (Charlestown), and the Skin Cancer and Cosmetic Clinic (Neutral Bay). The dataset consists of in vivo laser-induced plasma spectroscopy (LIPS) spectra acquired from suspicious skin lesions, with ground truth labels provided by histopathology (for biopsied lesions) or visual confirmation by physicians (for non-biopsied benign lesions).

use.training: Used for training the deep neural network (DNN) via 10-fold cross-validation. In each round, spectral data from 9 subject groups were used for training.
use.validation: Used for validation/evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation. In each round, spectral data from the remaining 1 subject group were used for evaluation.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation via 10-fold cross-validation. The total test set comprised 612 lesions from 353 patients across all 10 rounds.
use.other: Used for safety evaluation (histopathology of irradiated sites) and sensitivity analysis by cancer subtype.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the curve (AUC) of the receiver operating characteristic curve

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the diagnostic performance of the deep neural network (DNN) algorithm by partitioning the data into 10 equal-sized groups, using 9 for training and 1 for testing in each round, ensuring no overlap between training and test sets per subject.
To evaluate the diagnostic accuracy of the LIPS-DNN system in differentiating skin cancers from benign lesions.
To estimate the uncertainty of the sensitivity (94.6%) and specificity (88.9%) estimates.
To measure the area under the curve (AUC) for the diagnostic performance of the DNN-based algorithm.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance specifically for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), and Melanoma.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Diagnostic performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and 95% confidence intervals derived from 10-fold cross-validation.

Multisite Clinical Study Dataset (Austra.metric: Sensitivity and Specificity for differentiating skin cancers (BCC, SCC, Melanoma) from benign lesions
Multisite Clinical Study Dataset (Austra.notes: Results derived from 10-fold cross-validation on 612 lesions from 353 patients. Sensitivity varied from 90.3% to 96.7% across rounds; specificity varied from 83.6% to 100% across rounds. Threshold for malignancy probability was set at 0.5.

Sensitivity Analysis by Cancer Subtype.notes: Based on 296 malignant lesions total (186 BCC, 96 SCC, 14 Melanoma). True positives: 177 for BCC, 90 for SCC, 13 for Melanoma. False negatives: 9 for BCC, 6 for SCC, 1 for Melanoma.

Confusion Matrix Analysis (All Rounds).notes: Total test lesions: 612. When excluding visually confirmed benign lesions, true negatives decreased to 146 and false positives decreased to 19. Specificity with biopsied benign lesions only was 88.48%.

architecture_details.description: A multi-input DNN was designed for skin cancer diagnosis. The architecture was developed by comparing performance evaluations by varying network components, such as the number of layers (e.g., multi-input DNN with more layers) and the number of input types (e.g., single-input DNN using raw signal only).
architecture_details.input_data: Nondiscrete molecular Laser-Induced Plasma Spectroscopy (LIPS) spectra. Spectra were normalized to their spectral areas under the curve and interpolated with 0.125 nm resolution in the range of 270 to 800 nm.

training_details.data_splitting: Data split such that spectral data of the same patient did not belong to both training and test sets. In each round, 9 subject groups were used for training and 1 for evaluation.

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Thresholded at 0.5 to determine class label (malignant vs. benign)

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of nondiscrete molecular LIPS spectra to their spectral areas under the curve to minimize errors due to shot-to-shot intensity variation
Interpolation of spectra with 0.125 nm resolution in the range of 270 to 800 nm
conclusions: The LIPS system combined with a DNN-based diagnostic algorithm is a promising tool to distinguish skin cancers from benign lesions with high diagnostic accuracy in real clinical settings.
The system achieved a sensitivity of 94.6% (95% CI: 92.0%-97.2%) and a specificity of 88.9% (95% CI: 85.5%-92.4%) for differentiating skin cancers from benign lesions.
The procedure was found to be safe, with no adverse events, macroscopic marks, pigmentation, or histological evidence of tissue damage observed after laser irradiation.
The diagnostic performance was evaluated using a limited data set, and the authors acknowledge that more extensive clinical studies are needed to validate these results.

The study samples were recruited exclusively from Australia, which limits the generalizability of the results, and further research with external testing data sets is needed to strengthen generalizability.
The system is intended to provide information useful for medical professionals in biopsy decisions and is not intended to replace expert opinion or make a definitive diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study population was recruited exclusively from three clinics in Australia, which has the highest skin cancer rate in the world, limiting generalizability to other regions.

The sample consisted predominantly of light-skinned individuals (Fitzpatrick skin types I-IV, mostly II and III), with no representation of Fitzpatrick skin types V and VI, limiting applicability to darker skin tones.
The diagnostic performance was evaluated using a limited dataset (353 patients, 612 lesions), and the authors acknowledge that more extensive clinical studies are needed to validate these results.
The 10-fold cross-validation method carries a risk of overfitting due to the relatively limited data set size.
A significant portion of benign lesions (151 out of 316, or 47.8%) were confirmed visually by physicians rather than by histopathology, introducing potential label noise in the benign class.
The device is intended as an aid for biopsy decisions and is not intended to replace expert opinion or make a definitive diagnosis.
The study demonstrates high safety with no adverse events, macroscopic marks, or histological evidence of tissue damage observed after laser irradiation.
The proportion of biopsied lesions in the study may not reflect general dermatological practices, as many benign lesions were not biopsied.
